generatedSentence: Old Elara, an oracle of forgotten lore, sat, the scent of fresh onion and garlic filling her humble dwelling. She recounted tales of ancient voyages and the genesis of the realm to her subjects, dismissively noting the blatant historical inaccuracies peddled by the braindead scholars who wore a thin veneer of intelligence. Her true concern was the encroaching magical vacuum that threatened all alternatives to mundane existence. Rumors spoke of winged wyverns nesting in the shadowed foliage of the Forbidden Peaks, and a reclusive hater of peace who kept a pet pigeon with an unusually upright posture. She recalled how the Normans of legend had once used ice magic, a feat of such impiety it led to multiple impeachments among the elder council. "Their arrogance was nailed to history," she muttered, stroking a miniature rhino figurine, a gift from a traveling violin virtuoso. Old Elara watched as a colossal vulture circled, a harbinger of the approaching werewolves that made the ancient ox herds go haywire. A rogue magical nanny, a copycat of the ancient sorcerers, had taken up dark photography, capturing images of illusionary lobster-demons. This dark acceptor of chaos had recently sold a powerful simulation spell, distorting reality around the old windmill. Elara, holding her glowing torch, recalled tales of her grandfathers bravely driving back such threats. She had to imagine their courage, especially as the air filled with the scent of orange and tropical papayas from a nearby fruit stand, an oval of peace in the encroaching madness. The wizard, a true neutron of malice, was noisily kicking up dust, his kilt flapping crazily, planning to convert the valley into a war zone. She knew the time for random acts of heroism was past; a decisive release was needed. The wizard's lair was protected by ants the size of small dogs, enchanted to cause extreme elongation of shadows, making passage perilous. Elara, no longer merely welcoming fate, felt the weight of those she had wronged in her youth, now spurred to action. She donned a foil-lined helmet, recalling the tales of ancient witches and their potent work with a wand. She ducked through a shimmering portal, a temporary tear in spacetime, maintaining her spiritual uptime. A single freckle on her cheek seemed to pulse, a fractured connection to an old spell. She devoured a hastily prepared flapjack, fortifying herself for the trial. Her new idea, a kilo of pure magical bleach, was designed to rinse away the wizard's illusions, turning his lair into a barren raceway of truth, like a glitched videogame world. With a final voucher of courage, she prepared to deploy her arcane aeroplane. The wizard's reign was over, his destructive adjective-laden threats silenced. Elara found him wrecking the last of his magical devices, muttering wrongly about grand plans. She felt weary but triumphant. Among the debris, she saw a single daisy pushing through cracked pavement, a testament to resilience. The former spellbound maids of the castle, their magical fiber now restored, began to greet her. Even an elderly kitty emerged from behind a crumbling barn, purring with renewed benevolence. The wizard, an old fisherman now stripped of his power, could only offer a pathetic lump of apology. It marked a new epoch, a temporary lull before the next great challenge. Her tired bones ached, but the twenty years of unrest were over. True democracy had been restored, not just empty mickeys. The unbroken octagon symbol of peace now adorned the city gates. She picked up a child's forgotten wooden toy, a simple reminder of the innocence she had fought to protect, ensuring future generations could thrive without fear.
panelSentences: Old Elara, an oracle of forgotten lore, sat, the scent of fresh onion and garlic filling her humble dwelling. She recounted tales of ancient voyages and the genesis of the realm to her subjects, dismissively noting the blatant historical inaccuracies peddled by the braindead scholars who wore a thin veneer of intelligence. Her true concern was the encroaching magical vacuum that threatened all alternatives to mundane existence. Rumors spoke of winged wyverns nesting in the shadowed foliage of the Forbidden Peaks, and a reclusive hater of peace who kept a pet pigeon with an unusually upright posture. She recalled how the Normans of legend had once used ice magic, a feat of such impiety it led to multiple impeachments among the elder council. "Their arrogance was nailed to history," she muttered, stroking a miniature rhino figurine, a gift from a traveling violin virtuoso.

Old Elara watched as a colossal vulture circled, a harbinger of the approaching werewolves that made the ancient ox herds go haywire. A rogue magical nanny, a copycat of the ancient sorcerers, had taken up dark photography, capturing images of illusionary lobster-demons. This dark acceptor of chaos had recently sold a powerful simulation spell, distorting reality around the old windmill. Elara, holding her glowing torch, recalled tales of her grandfathers bravely driving back such threats. She had to imagine their courage, especially as the air filled with the scent of orange and tropical papayas from a nearby fruit stand, an oval of peace in the encroaching madness. The wizard, a true neutron of malice, was noisily kicking up dust, his kilt flapping crazily, planning to convert the valley into a war zone.

She knew the time for random acts of heroism was past; a decisive release was needed. The wizard's lair was protected by ants the size of small dogs, enchanted to cause extreme elongation of shadows, making passage perilous. Elara, no longer merely welcoming fate, felt the weight of those she had wronged in her youth, now spurred to action. She donned a foil-lined helmet, recalling the tales of ancient witches and their potent work with a wand. She ducked through a shimmering portal, a temporary tear in spacetime, maintaining her spiritual uptime. A single freckle on her cheek seemed to pulse, a fractured connection to an old spell. She devoured a hastily prepared flapjack, fortifying herself for the trial. Her new idea, a kilo of pure magical bleach, was designed to rinse away the wizard's illusions, turning his lair into a barren raceway of truth, like a glitched videogame world. With a final voucher of courage, she prepared to deploy her arcane aeroplane.

The wizard's reign was over, his destructive adjective-laden threats silenced. Elara found him wrecking the last of his magical devices, muttering wrongly about grand plans. She felt weary but triumphant. Among the debris, she saw a single daisy pushing through cracked pavement, a testament to resilience. The former spellbound maids of the castle, their magical fiber now restored, began to greet her. Even an elderly kitty emerged from behind a crumbling barn, purring with renewed benevolence. The wizard, an old fisherman now stripped of his power, could only offer a pathetic lump of apology. It marked a new epoch, a temporary lull before the next great challenge. Her tired bones ached, but the twenty years of unrest were over. True democracy had been restored, not just empty mickeys. The unbroken octagon symbol of peace now adorned the city gates. She picked up a child's forgotten wooden toy, a simple reminder of the innocence she had fought to protect, ensuring future generations could thrive without fear.
rawPanelSentences: Old Elara, an **oracle** of forgotten lore, sat, the scent of fresh **onion** and **garlic** filling her humble dwelling. She recounted tales of ancient **voyages** and the **genesis** of the realm to her **subjects**, dismissively noting the **blatant** historical inaccuracies peddled by the **braindead** scholars who wore a thin **veneer** of intelligence. Her true concern was the encroaching magical **vacuum** that threatened all **alternatives** to mundane existence. Rumors spoke of winged **wyverns** nesting in the shadowed **foliage** of the Forbidden Peaks, and a reclusive **hater** of peace who kept a pet **pigeon** with an unusually upright **posture**. She recalled how the **Normans** of legend had once used **ice** magic, a feat of such **impiety** it led to multiple **impeachments** among the elder council. "Their arrogance was **nailed** to history," she muttered, stroking a miniature **rhino** figurine, a gift from a traveling **violin** virtuoso.

Old Elara watched as a colossal **vulture** circled, a harbinger of the approaching **werewolves** that made the ancient **ox** herds go **haywire**. A rogue magical **nanny**, a **copycat** of the ancient sorcerers, had taken up dark **photography**, capturing images of illusionary **lobster**-demons. This dark **acceptor** of chaos had recently **sold** a powerful **simulation** spell, distorting reality around the old **windmill**. Elara, holding her glowing **torch**, recalled tales of her **grandfathers** bravely **driving** back such threats. She had to **imagine** their courage, especially as the air filled with the scent of **orange** and tropical **papayas** from a nearby fruit stand, an **oval** of peace in the encroaching madness. The wizard, a true **neutron** of malice, was **noisily** **kicking** up dust, his **kilt** flapping **crazily**, planning to **convert** the valley into a war zone.

She knew the time for **random** acts of heroism was past; a decisive **release** was needed. The wizard's lair was protected by **ants** the size of small dogs, enchanted to cause extreme **elongation** of shadows, making passage perilous. Elara, no longer merely **welcoming** fate, felt the weight of those she had **wronged** in her youth, now spurred to action. She donned a **foil**-lined **helmet**, recalling the tales of ancient **witches** and their potent **work** with a **wand**. She **ducked** through a shimmering **portal**, a temporary tear in spacetime, maintaining her spiritual **uptime**. A single **freckle** on her cheek seemed to pulse, a **fractured** connection to an old spell. She devoured a hastily prepared **flapjack**, fortifying herself for the trial. Her new **idea**, a **kilo** of pure magical **bleach**, was designed to **rinse** away the wizard's illusions, turning his lair into a barren **raceway** of truth, like a glitched **videogame** world. With a final **voucher** of courage, she prepared to deploy her arcane **aeroplane**.

The wizard's reign was over, his destructive **adjective**-laden threats silenced. Elara found him **wrecking** the last of his magical devices, muttering **wrongly** about grand plans. She felt **weary** but triumphant. Among the debris, she saw a single **daisy** pushing through cracked pavement, a testament to resilience. The former spellbound **maids** of the castle, their magical **fiber** now restored, began to **greet** her. Even an elderly **kitty** emerged from **behind** a crumbling **barn**, purring with renewed **benevolence**. The wizard, an old **fisherman** now stripped of his power, could only offer a pathetic **lump** of apology. It marked a new **epoch**, a **temporary** lull before the next great challenge. Her **tired** bones ached, but the **twenty** years of unrest were over. True **democracy** had been restored, not just empty **mickeys**. The **unbroken** **octagon** symbol of peace now adorned the city gates. She picked up a **child**'s forgotten wooden toy, a simple reminder of the innocence she had fought to protect, ensuring future generations could thrive without fear.
imagePrompt: A professional 4-panel comic book page, 2x2 grid, sequential art, gritty fantasy comic book art style, cel-shading, bold ink outlines, cinematic lighting, thick white gutters, clean black borders. A grizzled, middle-aged woman with a long scar across her cheek and silver streaks in her braided hair, wearing worn expedition gear, is the main character. TOP-LEFT PANEL: The grizzled woman, Elara, sits thoughtfully amidst ancient scrolls and mystical artifacts in a dimly lit, cozy study, stroking a small rhino figurine on a wooden table. Close-up shot. TOP-RIGHT PANEL: Elara stands defiantly on a windswept, barren plain, holding a glowing torch, facing a swirling illusion of monstrous lobster-like demons. Medium shot. BOTTOM-LEFT PANEL: Elara, wearing a foil-lined helmet, leaps through a shimmering, chaotic portal, sparks flying around her as she enters a new dimension. Dynamic low-angle shot. BOTTOM-RIGHT PANEL: Elara, weary but calm, stands in a restored, peaceful town square, picking up a child's wooden toy from the clean cobblestones, a gentle smile on her face. Eye-level shot. NO text, NO speech bubbles, NO dialogue, NO children.

definitions.definition: A monocotyledonous plant (Allium cepa), allied to garlic, used as vegetable and spice.
The bulb of such a plant.
The genus as a whole.
A ball.
A person from Bermuda or of Bermudian descent.
A shrine dedicated to some prophetic deity.
A person such as a priest through whom the deity is supposed to respond with prophecy or advice.
A prophetic response, often enigmatic or allegorical, so given.
A person considered to be a source of wisdom.
definitions.example: a literary oracle

definitions.definition: A wise sentence or decision of great authority.
One who communicates a divine command; an angel; a prophet.
A theoretical entity capable of answering some collection of questions.
(Jewish antiquity) The sanctuary, or most holy place in the temple; also, the temple itself.
To utter oracles or prophecies.
The state of having gas, often smelly, trapped (and when released, frequently with noise) in the digestive system of a human and some other animals; wind; and when released, a flatus, a fart.
The release of such gas; breaking wind.
(grammar) In a clause: the word or word group (usually a noun phrase) that is dealt with. In active clauses with verbs denoting an action, the subject and the actor are usually the same.
definitions.example: In the sentence ‘The mouse is eaten by the cat in the kitchen.’, ‘The mouse’ is the subject, ‘the cat’ being the agent.

definitions.definition: An actor; one who takes action.
definitions.example: The subjects and objects of power.

definitions.definition: The main topic of a paper, work of art, discussion, field of study, etc.
A particular area of study.
definitions.example: Her favorite subject is physics.

definitions.definition: A citizen in a monarchy.
definitions.example: I am a British subject.

definitions.definition: A person ruled over by another, especially a monarch or state authority.
The main theme or melody, especially in a fugue.
A human, animal or an inanimate object that is being examined, treated, analysed, etc.
A being that has subjective experiences, subjective consciousness, or a relationship with another entity.
That of which something is stated.
The variable in terms of which an expression is defined.
definitions.example: Making x the subject of x2 − 6x + 3y = 0, we have x = 3 ± √(9 − 3y).

definitions.definition: Normie
A wooden bar, or iron pin.
Bellowing; disagreeably clamorous; sounding loudly and harshly.
Obvious, on show; unashamed; loudly obtrusive or offensive.
Having an irreversible loss of brain function and cessation of brain activity.
Having no useful thoughts; stupid; ditzy.
A mythical beast having a horse's body with a man's head and torso in place of the head and neck of the horse.
(also capitalised) An icy planetoid that orbits the Sun between Jupiter and Neptune.
A chess-playing team comprising a human player and a computer who work together.
A thin decorative covering of fine material (usually wood) applied to coarser wood or other material.
An attractive appearance that covers or disguises true nature or feelings.
To apply veneer to.
definitions.example: to veneer a piece of furniture with mahogany

definitions.definition: To disguise with apparent goodness.
A region of space that contains no matter.
(plural only "vacuums") A vacuum cleaner.
The condition of rarefaction, or reduction of pressure below that of the atmosphere, in a vessel, such as the condenser of a steam engine, which is nearly exhausted of air or steam, etc.
definitions.example: a vacuum of 26 inches of mercury, or 13 pounds per square inch

definitions.definition: A spacetime having tensors of zero magnitude
To clean (something) with a vacuum cleaner.
To use a vacuum cleaner.
To optimise a database or database table by physically removing deleted tuples.
A situation which allows a mutually exclusive choice between two or more possibilities; a choice between two or more possibilities.
One of several mutually exclusive things which can be chosen.
The remaining option; something available after other possibilities have been exhausted.
Alternative rock
A draconian creature possessing wings, only two legs and usually a barbed tail.
The leaves of plants.
(short for) Fall foliage.
An architectural ornament representing foliage.
One who hates.
One who expresses unfounded or inappropriate hatred or dislike, particularly if motivated by envy.
One of several birds of the family Columbidae, which consists of more than 300 species.
The meat from this bird.
A person who is a target or victim of a confidence game.
Concern or responsibility.
definitions.example: it's his/her pigeon

definitions.definition: A pacifist, appeaser, an isolationist, a dove.
To deceive with a confidence game.
The way a person holds and positions their body.
A situation or condition.
One's attitude or the social or political position one takes towards an issue or another person.
The position of someone or something relative to another; position; situation.
To put one's body into a posture or series of postures, especially hoping that one will be noticed and admired
definitions.example: If you're finished posturing in front of the mirror, can I use the bathroom now?

definitions.definition: To pretend to have an opinion or a conviction
definitions.example: The politicians couldn't really care less about the issue: they're just posturing for the media.

definitions.definition: To place in a particular position or attitude; to pose.
definitions.example: to posture oneself; to posture a model

definitions.definition: Water in frozen (solid) form.
Any frozen volatile chemical, such as ammonia or carbon dioxide.
Any volatile chemical, such as water, ammonia, or carbon dioxide, not necessarily in solid form.
A frozen dessert made of fruit juice, water and sugar.
An ice cream.
Any substance having the appearance of ice.
One or more diamonds and jewelry.
Crystal form of amphetamine-based drugs.
The area where a game of ice hockey is played.
Money paid as a bribe.
To cool with ice, as a beverage.
To become ice; to freeze.
To make icy; to freeze.
To murder.
To cover with icing (frosting made of sugar and milk or white of egg); to frost; as cakes, tarts, etc.
To put out a team for a match.
definitions.example: Milton Keynes have yet to ice a team this season

definitions.definition: To shoot the puck the length of the playing surface, causing a stoppage in play called icing.
definitions.example: If the Bruins ice the puck, the faceoff will be in their own zone.

definitions.definition: The state of being impious.
An impious act.
The lack of respect for a god or something sacred.
The act of calling into question or challenging the accuracy or propriety of something.
The state of being impeached.
Hindrance; impediment; obstruction.
A plant, Allium sativum, related to the onion, having a pungent bulb much used in cooking.
To flavour with garlic
The origin, start, or point at which something comes into being.
definitions.example: Some point to the creation of Magna Carta as the genesis of English common law.

definitions.definition: To fix (an object) to another object using a nail.
definitions.example: He nailed the placard to the post.

definitions.definition: To drive a nail.
definitions.example: He used the ax head for nailing.

definitions.definition: To stud or boss with nails, or as if with nails.
To catch.
To expose as a sham.
To accomplish (a task) completely and successfully.
definitions.example: I really nailed that test.

definitions.definition: To hit (a target) effectively with some weapon.
Of a male, to engage in sexual intercourse with.
To spike, as a cannon.
To nail down: to make certain, or confirm.
Having nails (often of a specified kind).
definitions.example: a red-nailed finger

definitions.definition: A rhinoceros.

A musical four-string instrument, generally played with a bow or by plucking the string, with the pitch set by pressing the strings at the appropriate place with the fingers; also any instrument of the violin family.
A violinist.
definitions.example: The first violin often plays the lead melody lines in a string quartet.

definitions.definition: To play on, or as if on, a violin.
A long journey, especially by ship.
The act or practice of travelling.
To go on a long journey.
Any of several carrion-eating birds of the families Accipitridae and Cathartidae.
A person who profits from the suffering of others.
definitions.example: Within ten minutes of the accident, the vultures appeared and were organizing lawsuits.

definitions.definition: To circle around one's target as if one were a vulture.
definitions.example: Rudy vultured when asking the girl out.

definitions.definition: A person who is transformed or can transform into a wolf or a wolflike human, often said to transform during a full moon.
An adult castrated male of cattle (B. taurus).
Any bovine animal (genus Bos). A neat, a beef.
Wire used to bind bales of hay.
Roughly-made, unsophisticated, decrepit (from the use of haywire for temporary repairs).
Behaving erratically or uncontrollably, especially of a machine or mechanical process; usually used with the verb "go".
definitions.example: It was working fine until it went haywire and wouldn't stop printing blank sheets.

definitions.definition: The art and technology of producing images on photosensitive surfaces, and its digital counterpart.
The occupation of taking (and often printing) photographs.
A child's nurse.
A grandmother.
A godmother.
A female goat.
To serve as a nanny.
To treat like a nanny's charges; to coddle.
One who imitates or plagiarizes others' work.
A criminal who imitates the crimes of another; specifically, a criminal who commits the same crime, especially a highly-publicized one, that has just been or recently committed by someone else.
definitions.example: a copycat strangler

definitions.definition: To act as a copycat; to copy in a shameless or derivative way
Imitative; unoriginal.
A crustacean of the Nephropidae family, dark green or blue-black in colour turning bright red when cooked, with a hard shell and claws, which is used as a seafood.
A crustacean of the Palinuridae family, pinkish red in colour, with a hard, spiny shell but no claws, which is used as a seafood.
A soldier or officer of the imperial British Army (due to their red or scarlet uniform).
An Australian twenty dollar note, due to its reddish-orange colour.
To fish for lobsters.
Red-colored, especially from a sunburn.
One who accepts.
One who accepts a draft or a bill of exchange; a drawee after he has accepted.
An atom or molecule which can accept an electron to form a chemical bond.
A transfer RNA molecule that can accept a specific amino acid
A chemical acceptor atom forming a positive hole in a semiconductor
A cluster of skin cells that respond to pain
A kind of finite-state machine whose binary output indicates whether or not a received input was accepted.
A machine which translates linear motion of wind to rotational motion by means of adjustable vanes called sails.
The structure containing such machinery.
A child's toy consisting of vanes mounted on a stick that rotate when blown by a person or by the wind.
A dunk where the dunker swings his arm in a circular motion before throwing the ball through the hoop.
A guitar move where the strumming hand mimics a turning windmill.
A breakdancing move in which the dancer rolls his/her torso continuously in a circular path on the floor, across the upper chest, shoulders and back, while twirling the legs in a V shape in the air.
Any of various large papilionid butterflies of the genus Byasa, the wings of which resemble the vanes of a windmill.
The false shower.
An imaginary enemy, but presented as real.
To rotate with a sweeping motion.
definitions.example: She ran down the hill, windmilling her arms with glee.

definitions.definition: Of a rotating part of a machine, to (become disengaged and) rotate freely.
definitions.example: The axle broke and the wheel windmilled in place briefly before careening through the wall.

definitions.definition: A stick with a flame on one end, used chiefly as a light source; a similarly shaped implement with a replaceable supply of flammable material.
definitions.example: The mob of angry villagers carried torches and pitchforks to the vampire's castle.

definitions.definition: A portable light source powered by electricity; a flashlight.
definitions.example: Ernst slipped and dropped his torch on the flagstones, shattering the bulb and plunging us into darkness.

definitions.definition: An arsonist.
A blowtorch or oxy-gas torch.
To set fire to, especially by use of a torch (flaming stick).
definitions.example: Some hoodlums had torched a derelict automobile, which emitted a ghastly pall of thick, black smoke that filled the street.

definitions.definition: To provide an impetus for motion or other physical change, to move an object by means of the provision of force thereto.
definitions.example: You drive nails into wood with a hammer.

definitions.definition: To provide an impetus for a non-physical change, especially a change in one's state of mind.
definitions.example: My wife's constant harping about the condition of the house threatens to drive me to distraction.

definitions.definition: To displace either physically or non-physically, through the application of force.
To cause intrinsic motivation through the application or demonstration of force: to impel or urge onward thusly, to compel to move on, to coerce, intimidate or threaten.
(especially of animals) To impel or urge onward by force; to push forward; to compel to move on.
definitions.example: to drive twenty thousand head of cattle from Texas to the Kansas railheads; to drive sheep out of a field

definitions.definition: To direct a vehicle powered by a horse, ox or similar animal.
To cause animals to flee out of.
definitions.example: The beaters drove the brambles, causing a great rush of rabbits and other creatures.

definitions.definition: To move (something) by hitting it with great force.
definitions.example: You drive nails into wood with a hammer.

definitions.definition: To cause (a mechanism) to operate.
definitions.example: The pistons drive the crankshaft.

definitions.definition: To operate (a wheeled motorized vehicle).
definitions.example: drive a car

definitions.definition: To motivate; to provide an incentive for.
definitions.example: What drives a person to run a marathon?

definitions.definition: To compel (to do something).
definitions.example: Their debts finally drove them to sell the business.

definitions.definition: To cause to become.
definitions.example: This constant complaining is going to drive me to insanity.   You are driving me crazy!

definitions.definition: To hit the ball with a drive.
To travel by operating a wheeled motorized vehicle.
definitions.example: I drive to work every day.

definitions.definition: To convey (a person, etc) in a wheeled motorized vehicle.
definitions.example: My wife drove me to the airport.

definitions.definition: To move forcefully.
To be moved or propelled forcefully (especially of a ship).
To urge, press, or bring to a point or state.
To carry or to keep in motion; to conduct; to prosecute.
To clear, by forcing away what is contained.
To dig horizontally; to cut a horizontal gallery or tunnel.
To put together a drive (n.): to string together offensive plays and advance the ball down the field.
To distrain for rent.
To separate the lighter (feathers or down) from the heavier, by exposing them to a current of air.
To be the dominant party in a sex act.
That drives (a mechanism or process).
Of wind, rain, etc That drives forcefully; strong; forceful; violent
An elongated round shape resembling an egg or ellipse.
A thing having such a shape, such as an arena.
In a projective plane, a set of points such that no three are collinear and there is a unique tangent line at each point.
Having the shape of an oval.
Of or pertaining to an ovum.
definitions.example: oval conceptions

definitions.definition: An evergreen tree of the genus Citrus such as Citrus sinensis.
The fruit of an orange tree; a citrus fruit with a slightly sour flavour.
The colour of a ripe fruit of an orange tree, midway between red and yellow.
Orange juice.
Orange coloured and flavoured cordial.
Orange coloured and flavoured soft drink.
To color orange.
To become orange.
Having the colour of the fruit of an orange tree; yellowred; reddish-yellow.
A tropical American evergreen tree, Carica papaya, having large, yellow, edible fruit
The fruit of this tree.
An orange colour, like that of papaya flesh.
A short fanfic or prompt placing a reader insert in a novel scenario with a character or celebrity.
To form a mental image of something; to envision or create something in one's mind.
definitions.example: Try to imagine a pink elephant.

definitions.definition: To believe in something created by one's own mind.
definitions.example: She imagined that the man wanted to kill her.

definitions.definition: To assume
definitions.example: I imagine that he will need to rest after such a long flight.

definitions.definition: To conjecture or guess
definitions.example: I cannot even imagine what you are up to!

definitions.definition: To use one's imagination
definitions.example: Imagine that we were siblings.

definitions.definition: To contrive in purpose; to scheme; to devise.
A father of someone's parent.
(by extension) A male forefather.
To be, or act as, a grandfather to.
To retain discontinued laws or rules for (a thing, person or organization previously affected by them).
(ditransitive) To transfer goods or provide services in exchange for money.
definitions.example: I'll sell you all three for a hundred dollars.

definitions.definition: To be sold.
definitions.example: The corn sold for a good price.

definitions.definition: To promote a product or service.
To promote a particular viewpoint.
definitions.example: My boss is very old-fashioned and I'm having a lot of trouble selling the idea of working at home occasionally.

definitions.definition: To betray for money.
To trick, cheat, or manipulate someone.
To pretend that an opponent's blows or maneuvers are causing legitimate injury; to act.
Something that simulates a system or environment in order to predict actual behaviour.
definitions.example: The most reliable simulation predicts that the hurricane will turn north.

definitions.definition: The process of simulating.
definitions.example: Despite extensive simulation in the design phase, the aircraft failed to behave as expected.

definitions.definition: A video game designed to convey a more or less realistic experience, as of a sport or warfare.
definitions.example: Some kids in the classroom will be playing simulations after school.

definitions.definition: Assuming an appearance which is feigned, or not true.
The act of falling over in order to be awarded a foul, when no foul has been committed.
A subatomic particle forming part of the nucleus of an atom and having no charge; it is a combination of an up quark and two down quarks.
In a noisy manner; in such a way as to create a great deal of noise or sound
definitions.example: They talked noisily and long into the night.

definitions.definition: To strike or hit with the foot or other extremity of the leg.
definitions.example: Did you kick your brother?

definitions.definition: To make a sharp jerking movement of the leg, as to strike something.
definitions.example: He enjoyed the simple pleasure of watching the kickline kick.

definitions.definition: To direct to a particular place by a blow with the foot or leg.
definitions.example: Kick the ball into the goal.

definitions.definition: (with "off" or "out") To eject summarily.
To forcibly remove a participant from an online activity.
definitions.example: He was kicked by ChanServ for flooding.

definitions.definition: To overcome (a bothersome or difficult issue or obstacle); to free oneself of (a problem).
definitions.example: I still smoke, but they keep telling me to kick the habit.

definitions.definition: To move or push suddenly and violently.
definitions.example: He was kicked sideways by the force of the blast.

definitions.definition: (of a firearm) To recoil; to push by recoiling.
To attack (a piece) in order to force it to move.
To accelerate quickly with a few pedal strokes in an effort to break away from other riders.
definitions.example: Contador kicks again to try to rid himself of Rasmussen.

definitions.definition: To show opposition or resistance.
To work a press by impact of the foot on a treadle.
To die.
The action of the verb to kick.
definitions.example: In boxing, kicking one's opponent is not allowed.

definitions.definition: A violent assault involving repeated kicks.
definitions.example: The bullies pushed the boy over and gave him a kicking.

definitions.definition: A great loss or defeat; licking.
Terrific, great (of clothes) smart, fashionable.
definitions.example: a kicking pair of jeans

definitions.definition: Alive, active (especially in the phrase alive and kicking).
definitions.example: still kicking at 89

definitions.definition: (of a party or event) Actively ongoing and enjoyable.

A traditional Scottish garment, usually worn by men, having roughly the same morphology as a wrap-around skirt, with overlapping front aprons and pleated around the sides and back, and usually made of twill-woven worsted wool with a tartan pattern.
Any Scottish garment from which the above lies in a direct line of descent, such as the philibeg, or the great kilt or belted plaid
A plaid, pleated school uniform skirt sometimes structured as a wrap around, sometimes pleated throughout the entire circumference; also used as boys' wear in 19th century USA.
A variety of non-bifurcated garments made for men and loosely resembling a Scottish kilt, but most often made from different fabrics and not always with tartan plaid designs.
To gather up (skirts) around the body.
In a crazy manner.
Very, extremely.
A person who has converted to a religion.
definitions.example: They were all converts to Islam.

definitions.definition: A person who is now in favour of something that he or she previously opposed or disliked.
definitions.example: I never really liked broccoli before, but now that I've tasted it the way you cook it, I'm a convert!

definitions.definition: The equivalent of a conversion in rugby
To transform or change (something) into another form, substance, state, or product.
definitions.example: A kettle converts water into steam.

definitions.definition: To change (something) from one use, function, or purpose to another.
definitions.example: He converted his garden into a tennis court.

definitions.definition: To induce (someone) to adopt a particular religion, faith, ideology or belief (see also sense 11).
definitions.example: They converted her to Roman Catholicism on her deathbed.

definitions.definition: To exchange for something of equal value.
definitions.example: We converted our pounds into euros.

definitions.definition: To express (a quantity) in alternative units.
To express (a unit of measurement) in terms of another; to furnish a mathematical formula by which a quantity, expressed in the former unit, may be given in the latter.
definitions.example: How do you convert feet into metres?

definitions.definition: To appropriate wrongfully or unlawfully; to commit the common law tort of conversion.
(rugby football) To score extra points after (a try) by completing a conversion.
To score (especially a penalty kick).
(ten-pin bowling) To score a spare.
To undergo a conversion of religion, faith or belief (see also sense 3).
definitions.example: We’ve converted to Methodism.

definitions.definition: To become converted.
definitions.example: The chair converts into a bed.

definitions.definition: To cause to turn; to turn.
To change (one proposition) into another, so that what was the subject of the first becomes the predicate of the second.
To turn into another language; to translate.
To increase one's individual score, especially from 50 runs (a fifty) to 100 runs (a century), or from a century to a double or triple century.
To perform the action that an online advertisement is intended to induce; to reach the point of conversion.
definitions.example: Each time a user clicks on one of your adverts, you will be charged the bid amount whether the user converts or not.

definitions.definition: A roving motion; course without definite direction; lack of rule or method; chance.
Speed, full speed; impetuosity, force.
The full range of a bullet or other projectile; hence, the angle at which a weapon is tilted to allow the greatest range.
An undefined, unknown or unimportant person; a person of no consequence.

definitions.definition: The direction of a rake-vein.
Having unpredictable outcomes and, in the ideal case, all outcomes equally probable; resulting from such selection; lacking statistical correlation.
definitions.example: The flip of a fair coin is purely random.

definitions.definition: Of or relating to probability distribution.
definitions.example: A toss of loaded dice is still random, though biased.

definitions.definition: Pseudorandom; mimicking the result of random selection.
(somewhat colloquial) Representative and undistinguished; typical and average; selected for no particular reason.
definitions.example: A random American off the street couldn't tell the difference.

definitions.definition: (somewhat colloquial) Apropos of nothing; lacking context; unexpected; having apparent lack of plan, cause or reason.

definitions.definition: Characterized by or often saying random things; habitually using non sequiturs.

definitions.definition: The event of setting (someone or something) free (e.g. hostages, slaves, prisoners, caged animals, hooked or stuck mechanisms).
The distribution of an initial or new and upgraded version of a computer software product; the distribution can be either public or private.
Anything recently released or made available (as for sale).

definitions.definition: That which is released, untied or let go.

definitions.definition: The giving up of a claim, especially a debt.
Liberation from pain or suffering.
The process by which a chemical substance is set free.

In the block system, a printed card conveying information and instructions to be used at intermediate sidings without telegraphic stations.
A device adapted to hold or release a device or mechanism as required.

definitions.definition: To free or liberate; to set free.

definitions.definition: To discharge.
